zh.lifeSummary: Thomas Herman於1332年出生在英國英格蘭素可。他至少有1個女兒。

nameMeanings.name: Herman
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English, French, Walloon, Dutch, Flemish, Hungarian, Polish, Czech, Slovak, Slovenian, and Croatian: from a personal name composed of the ancient Germanic elements heri, hari ‘army’ + man ‘man’. Compare Harman 5.
Americanized form of German   Hermann  , a cognate of 1 above.
English: perhaps a shortened form of Herniman, a topographic name for a ‘dweller (in a) nook or corner of land’, from Middle English herne, hirne, hurne + man. See also Hearn .

variants.name: Hermansen
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Scandinavian Erik, Knud, Alf, Knut.  German Hans, Inge, Kurt, Markus.
Danish and Norwegian: patronymic from the personal name   Herman  .
North German: variant of Hermannsen, a patronymic from the personal name Hermann . Compare Hermanson .

variants.name: Harjes
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Hermann, Oskar.

North German, East Frisian, and North Frisian: patronymic from a derivative of any of the ancient Germanic personal names formed with hari, heri ‘army’ as the first element (see for example Herman , Herwig ). This surname is very rare in North Frisia.

variants.name: Hermon
meanings: English (Berkshire): variant of   Herman  .

variants.name: Harke
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Gitta, Guenter, Heinz.

North German and North Frisian: from a Frisian personal name, a short form of any of the ancient Germanic personal names with hari, heri ‘army’ as the first element (see for example Herman , Herwig ).
North German: possibly a nickname or a metonymic occupational name from Middle Low German harke ‘rake’, or a topographic name from herk, hark ‘marsh, bog’.

variants.name: Hermes
meanings: German and Dutch: patronymic from a short form of the personal name   Herman  . This surname is also found in France (Lorraine).
French (Hermès): topographic name for someone who lived in a deserted spot or on a patch of waste land, from Occitan erm ‘desert, waste’ (from Greek erēmia) + the topographic suffix -ès.

French (Hermès): in some cases, possibly also from the personal name Hermès, from Greek Hermēs, the name of a Greek god and also of a Christian saint mentioned briefly in Paul's Epistle to the Romans.

variants.name: Hermsen
meanings: Dutch and North German: patronymic from a short form of the personal name Herman + -sen ‘son’.

variants.name: Harman
meanings: English (mainly southeast), Dutch, and Czech: from a personal name composed of the ancient Germanic elements heri, hari ‘army’ + man ‘man’ (see Hermann ). In England this name was introduced by the Normans.
Irish: variant of   Hargadon  .
Jewish (eastern Ashkenazic): perhaps a nickname for someone with a copious or noticeable head of hair (see   Haar  ).

variants.name: Hermanson
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Scandinavian Erik, Helmer, Anders, Bjorn, Per.
Americanized form (and a rare Swedish variant) of Swedish Hermansson: patronymic from the personal name Herman .
Americanized form of Danish and Norwegian Hermansen , a cognate of 1 above, and of its rare variant Hermannsen, which is also North German.

variants.name: Hermans
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  French Albertine, Amiee, Anais, Andre, Emile, Michel.
Dutch and Flemish: patronymic from   Herman  .

variants.name: Herko
meanings: Slovak and Polish: probably from a short form of originally German personal names beginning with Her-, e.g. Herman .

nameMeanings.name: Thomas
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: New Testament name, borne by one of Christ's twelve apostles, referred to as ‘Thomas, called Didymus’ (John 11:16; 20:24). Didymos is the Greek word for ‘twin’, and the name is the Greek form of an Aramaic byname meaning ‘twin’. The given name has always been popular throughout Christendom, in part because St Thomas's doubts have made him seem a very human character.
